grammar.usage: Used to report what another person has said by making appropriate changes to tenses, pronouns, and time/place references.
grammar.example: He said that she always wore a coat. / He told me he hadn't finished his work.

bullets: We use reported speech when we want to report what another person said.
Usually, the tenses shift back into the past when the reporting verb is in the past tense.

rows: He said (that) she always wore a coat.
She said that she was looking for her keys.
"I have been drawing all day," said Tom.
Tom said he had been drawing all day.
"My friend gave me a bar of chocolate yesterday."
She said her friend had given her a bar of chocolate the day before.
"I was writing a poem," said Tina.
Tina said she had been writing a poem.
"I will finish my report in two days."
He said that he would finish his report in two days.
"They are going to make a new one," said Joe.
Joe said they were going to make a new one.
"I can draw quite well," Emma said.
Emma said she could draw quite well.
"You have to visit the fashion show," he said.
He said we had to visit the fashion show.
"I may visit the Thien Mu Pagoda next week," said Hong.
Hong said she might visit the Thien Mu Pagoda the following week.

bullets: - **last week** -> **the week before / the previous week**
**Rule 1: Reporting verb in the present tense**
- If the reporting verb is in the present tense (e.g. *says, tells*), the tense in reported speech does not change.
- EX: *Tom says, "I am going to study architecture."* -> **Tom says he is going to study architecture.**
- Past perfect simple and past perfect continuous do not shift tense in reported speech.
- EX: *"I had seen the picture before," he said.* -> **He said he had seen the picture before.**
- Modal verbs like *would, should, could, might* do not change form.
- EX: *"I might take up painting," she said.* -> **She said she might take up painting.**
- You can use specific reporting verbs (e.g. *apologize, deny, promise, refuse*) to summarize the speaker's words.
- EX: *"I'm sorry I told everyone your secret," my sister said.* -> **My sister apologized for telling everyone my secret.**
- EX: *"I'll love you forever!" Francis said to Elizabeth.* -> **Francis promised to love Elizabeth forever.**
- EX: *"No, I won't open the door!" said Mandy.* -> **Mandy refused to open the door.**
- EX: *"I didn't give the money to John," said Ali.* -> **Ali denied giving the money to John.**

rows:
S + told + O + (that) + S + V
S + said + (that) + S + V

EX: He said to me "I haven't finished my work."
-> **He told me he hadn't finished his work.**
S + asked/wanted to know/wondered + if/whether + S + V

EX: "Are you angry?" he asked.
-> **He asked if/whether I was angry.**
S + asked (+ O) / wanted to know / wondered + Wh-word + S + V

EX: "What are you talking about?" said the teacher.
-> **The teacher asked us what we were talking about.**

EX: "Please wait for me here, Mary," said Tom.
-> **Tom told Mary to wait for him there.**
S + told / ordered + O + not + to-infinitive

EX: "Don't talk in class," the teacher said to us.
-> **The teacher told us not to talk in class.**

vocabulary.example: They sat on a simple wooden bench in the garden.

vocabulary.meaning: a book or set of books giving information on many subjects
vocabulary.example: He looked up the history of Rome in the encyclopaedia.

vocabulary.meaning: the organ in your body where food is digested
vocabulary.example: He had a stomach ache after eating too much candy.

vocabulary.meaning: to continuously throw several objects into the air and catch them
vocabulary.example: The street performer could juggle five balls at the same time.

vocabulary.meaning: to win a point in a game or sport
vocabulary.example: She managed to score the winning goal in the final minute of the match.

vocabulary.meaning: a box-shaped container in a piece of furniture that slides in and out
vocabulary.example: He kept his socks and t-shirts in the top drawer of the dresser.
